planting.note: Root the runner plantlets or set divisions after all frost; often grown as a houseplant.

directions: Grow this variegated form in bright shade to bring out the pink, cream, and green leaf edges. It is more tender than the plain species and is often kept as a potted plant. Delicate sprays of small white flowers rise above the colorful rosettes in summer.

planting.note: Plant or divide crowns in spring in a sheltered, shaded bed.

directions: Grow in cool, moist, leafy soil in shade to show off the deep red-purple foliage. Shelter the plant from cold drying wind that can spoil the late display. Airy sprays of white, lopsided flowers open over the dark leaves in autumn.

planting.note: Plant rooted cushions into gritty, lime-rich scree in spring.

directions: Grow this tight silver cushion in gritty tufa or scree with sharp drainage and some shade. Keep the crown dry in winter, as standing wet quickly rots the dense hummock. A profusion of soft pink flowers on short stems smothers the cushion in very early spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cushions into sharply drained, lime-rich soil in spring.

directions: Grow the spiny silver cushion in gritty tufa or scree with faultless drainage. Give light shade in hot gardens and keep winter moisture off the crown. Unusually large solitary white flowers on short red stems appear in late winter to early spring.

planting.note: Divide and replant the spreading rosettes in spring.

directions: Plant the yellow-speckled evergreen rosettes in cool part shade for the best leaf color. It knits into a tough groundcover mat much like the plain London pride. Airy stems of pale pink, red-dotted flowers rise above the gold-flecked leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant or divide the variegated cushions in cool, moist spring soil.

directions: Grow this cream-variegated mossy form in cool part shade to keep the marbled foliage crisp. Water in dry spells, as the fine leaves scorch under hot dry sun. Short stems carry small white flowers above the frosted green cushions in late spring.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before, spacing clumps so air moves freely between them. Give lean, sharply drained soil and avoid rich feeding, which makes the stems flop. Broccoli-like flower heads open pink in late summer and deepen to rusty copper by fall; leave the dried heads standing for winter interest.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the sturdiest upright stems.

directions: Set plants in gritty, well-drained ground and water only until established. This improved selection holds its stems more stiffly than older forms, so staking is rarely needed. Rosy flower heads brighten to brick red in autumn and draw bees and butterflies well into fall.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors before the last frost, then transplant out after the danger passes.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of a gritty mix and do not cover it, since light aids germination. Move seedlings to lean soil and full sun where they form fleshy blue-green clumps. Flat clusters of pink star flowers open in late summer and are a magnet for pollinators.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring to keep clumps vigorous.

directions: Plant in poor to average soil that drains fast, and skip the fertilizer. Bright carmine-pink flower heads sit atop grey-green foliage and glow when backlit by autumn sun. Cut old stems back in late winter just before new rosettes push up.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a bright, open spot.

directions: Give this selection full sun and gritty drainage so the stems stay upright. It is prized for unusually vivid magenta-pink flower heads that read almost fluorescent in the fall garden. Divide clumps every three or four years to refresh their vigor and keep centers from opening.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its dark stems can be seen against lighter neighbors.

directions: Grow in lean, well-drained soil so the thick burgundy stems hold their tall, self-supporting habit. Its grey-green leaves are flushed with purple, topped by soft dusty-pink flower heads in late summer. Leave the seed heads for winter structure and cut them down in early spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring in full sun to develop the darkest leaf color.

directions: Plant in full sun and poor soil, since shade and rich feeding fade the foliage and weaken the stems. The deep beetroot-purple leaves are the main draw, contrasting with dusky-pink flower clusters in late summer. Pinch stems in early summer if you want a bushier, shorter plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Give full sun and gritty soil to bring out the smoky purple-black foliage. Rosy-pink flower heads open above the dark leaves in late summer and age to deeper tones. It stands more upright than many dark-leaved sedums, so it rarely needs support.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the richest flower color.

directions: Set in lean, fast-draining soil and water sparingly once rooted. This old Gertrude Jekyll selection carries deep brick-red flower heads over grey-green, purple-tinged foliage. Deadheading is unnecessary; the rusty seed heads look handsome through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a little afternoon shade in hot climates protects the white leaf edges.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and give steady sun, but shelter from harsh afternoon glare where summers are fierce. The grey-green leaves carry crisp white margins that give the plant a cool, frosted look. Pale pink flower heads open in early fall above the variegated foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and lean soil.

directions: This variegated sport of Autumn Joy grows on the same care: sun, sharp drainage, and no coddling. Blue-green leaves edged in creamy yellow build a tidy mound through summer. Dusty pink flower heads open in late summer and deepen to rose as the season cools.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for compact, upright clumps.

directions: Give full sun and gritty, well-drained soil to keep the habit dense and self-supporting. This selection is valued for large, rounded flower heads in deep purple-pink over sturdy grey-green foliage. Pollinators crowd the blooms from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant in lean soil and full sun to hold its low, tidy dome. It blooms earlier than most border sedums, opening broad white flower heads over neat green foliage. The clump is compact and floriferous, making it a good front-of-border choice.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the sturdiest stems.

directions: Set in gritty, well-drained soil and keep it on the dry side once established. Thick upright stems carry rounded rosy-pink flower heads that resist flopping. Divide every few years in spring to maintain vigor and flower size.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the smoky leaf color.

directions: This low, lax hybrid drapes nicely at a border edge or over a wall, so give it room to sprawl. The dusky purple-grey leaves pair with dusky-pink flower heads in late summer. Grow it hard in lean soil and full sun to deepen the foliage tones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Give this compact, spreading selection full sun to keep its dark plum foliage rich. Rounded heads of deep rose-red flowers appear in late summer above the low mound. It is excellent tumbling over the edge of a raised bed or trough.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its arching stems can cascade over an edge.

directions: Set in gritty soil and full sun where the trailing stems can spill over a rock or wall. Round blue-grey leaves are carried in whorls of three along arching stems that radiate from the center. Clusters of pink flowers open in autumn, later than most stonecrops, giving the plant its name.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny crevice or trough with sharp drainage.

directions: Tuck into gritty, fast-draining soil where its low mats can trail over stone. Small blue-grey leaves clothe wiry stems that turn upward at the tips. Rosy-pink flower clusters open in late summer and early fall, drawing bees to the rock garden.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; stem pieces root readily where they touch soil.

directions: Press rooted pieces into lean, gritty soil and firm them lightly; they knit into a dense mat quickly. Foliage emerges green and flushes deep bronze-red in sun and cool weather. Rosy-red star flowers cover the mat in summer, and the plant makes a tough groundcover for hot, dry banks.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for the deepest leaf color.

directions: Set into fast-draining soil and give full sun to intensify the maroon foliage. This selection holds richer, darker leaf color than most and stays compact. Deep red flowers open in mid to late summer above the low, spreading mat.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; remove any all-green shoots that appear.

directions: Grow in gritty soil and full sun to keep the variegation crisp. Small leaves are marbled green, cream, and pink, making an eye-catching low carpet. Pinch out any reverted green stems promptly, since they are more vigorous and can overtake the variegated growth; pale pink flowers appear in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a low, walkable groundcover.

directions: Press pieces into lean soil where they root quickly into a tight, flat mat. The small scalloped green leaves form one of the lowest and densest of the spurium types, tolerating light foot traffic between pavers. Pink flowers open late in the season above the evergreen carpet.

planting.note: Surface-sow or press divisions into gritty ground; it spreads readily.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface of gritty soil without covering, since it needs light to sprout. Tiny bright-green leaves form a mossy carpet that thrives in the poorest, driest sites, including green roofs and gravel. Golden-yellow star flowers blanket the mat in early summer; note the sap can irritate skin, so wash after handling.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring, or press stem pieces into gritty soil to root.

directions: Sow on the surface of lean, sharply drained soil and keep just moist until seedlings appear. The plump cylindrical leaves often blush red in sun and drought, forming a dense evergreen mat. Airy clusters of small white flowers rise above the foliage in summer and attract many small pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where cool weather can color the foliage.

directions: Set into gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun. New spring growth emerges bright green, turns coral-orange in summer heat, and deepens to red-bronze in winter. White summer flowers dot the low mat, which works beautifully in troughs and between stepping stones.

planting.note: Plant in spring; broken stems root wherever they land.

directions: Press rooted sprigs into lean, gritty soil where they spread fast into a golden carpet. The needle-like leaves are chartreuse in summer and take on amber and orange tones in cold weather. Yellow star flowers appear in summer, but the foliage is the real year-round attraction.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and dry, gritty soil.

directions: Grow in poor, well-drained soil and full sun, where the foliage looks like miniature blue-green conifer shoots. It creeps steadily into a soft carpet that shrugs off heat and drought. Yellow flowers rise on short stems in summer above the icy-blue mat.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out, or divide established clumps in spring.

directions: Surface-sow seed indoors on a gritty mix, or lift and split clumps in early spring. The toothed green leaves form a low mound rather than a flat mat and take on reddish tints in fall. Deep yellow star flowers open in early summer and give way to attractive orange-red seed capsules.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring; remove any plain-green shoots.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun to part sun. Toothed green leaves are edged in creamy white, and new growth is often flushed pink. Yellow flowers open in summer; pull out any fully green reverted shoots so they do not crowd out the variegated growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give light afternoon shade where summers are hot.

directions: Grow in well-drained but not bone-dry soil, since this species likes a touch more moisture than most stonecrops. The tiny rounded leaves glow golden-yellow, brightest with some afternoon shade in hot regions. Small yellow flowers appear in summer on the low, creeping mat, which is lovely in containers and shady edges.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharp drainage; part shade helps in hot climates.

directions: Set into very fast-draining, gritty soil, as this west-coast native resents wet feet. The spoon-shaped leaves are coated in silvery-white farina and packed into tight rosettes. Yellow flowers rise on short stems in early summer above the powder-blue mat.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty soil with excellent drainage.

directions: Grow in lean, sharply drained soil and avoid summer overwatering. The rosettes are dusted with pale bloom over rich purple leaves, giving a frosted lavender effect. Bright yellow flowers open in early summer and contrast strongly with the dark foliage.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in humus-rich soil under light shade.

directions: Unlike sun-loving stonecrops, this eastern North American native prefers humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in shade or dappled light. Whorls of three flat green leaves clothe low stems that root as they creep along the ground. Sprays of white star flowers open in spring, making it a fine groundcover for woodland gardens.

planting.note: Sow or set out in spring in gritty, moderately draining soil.

directions: This Pacific Northwest native grows in gritty soil and tolerates part shade better than many stonecrops. Glossy spoon-shaped leaves form low rosettes that flush bronze-red in sun and cold. Bright yellow flowers open in summer above the spreading evergreen mat.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring; it self-seeds where happy.

directions: Scatter seed on thin, gritty soil in fall or early spring, since this native of rocky glades often behaves as an annual or short-lived perennial. Fine, needle-like green leaves clothe low stems that turn red in dry heat. Pink flowers are arranged along arching branches that form a distinctive cross or pinwheel shape in late spring; let it self-sow to keep it in the garden.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring; it reseeds gently in gritty soil.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on lean, gritty soil and do not cover it. The slender blue-grey leaves form a low, ferny carpet that often self-sows to fill gaps in gravel and paving. Small pale-pink to white flowers open in summer; it may be short-lived but renews itself from seed.

planting.note: Plant after frost as a season-long filler; treat as an annual in cold zones.

directions: Set out after the last frost in containers or beds, where it forms a bright chartreuse mound of needle-like foliage. It is grown mainly for its glowing lime color and quickly spills over pot edges. Hardy only in warm zones, it is usually enjoyed as a tender annual and discarded or overwintered indoors elsewhere.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharp drainage.

directions: Grow in well-drained soil and full sun to keep the bold variegation crisp. Toothed green leaves carry wide creamy-yellow margins, and new spring rosettes emerge tinted pink. Yellow flowers open in early summer above the mounding, spreading foliage that stays attractive all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; part of the SunSparkler series.

directions: Set in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun to develop the best foliage color. This compact hybrid forms a low mound of smoky blue-grey leaves that turn purple in cool weather. Very large raspberry-red flower heads open in late summer and are a strong pollinator draw.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; part of the SunSparkler series.

directions: Plant in full sun and sharp drainage to bring out the cherry-red foliage. The low, dense mound holds its glossy red leaves all season, deepening in cool weather. Pink flowers cover the plant in late summer, making a tidy front-of-border groundcover.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; part of the SunSparkler series.

directions: Grow in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun. The rounded apple-green leaves are edged with a thin red margin, forming a bright low mound. Soft pink flowers open in late summer above the crisp, colorful foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; part of the SunSparkler series.

directions: Set into fast-draining soil in full sun for the richest leaf color. This low, spreading selection carries deep cherry-red foliage on a compact mound. Rosy-pink flowers open in summer, and the plant looks good tumbling over a wall or trough edge.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; part of the SunSparkler series.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun. It makes a very low, dense cushion of small blue-grey leaves that stays neat all season. Rosy-pink flowers open in late summer over the cool-toned foliage, ideal for edging and containers.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring; stems root readily along the ground.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed or press rooted stems into gritty soil, where they spread quickly into a tough mat. The rounded, scalloped green leaves make an excellent groundcover for slopes, walls, and hot dry sites. Pink star flowers cover the mat in summer and draw many bees.

planting.note: Sow or set out in spring in very sharply drained soil.

directions: This western North American native needs gritty, fast-draining soil and dislikes summer wet. Spoon-shaped leaves form silvery-blue rosettes that spread slowly into a low mat, often reddening in sun. Yellow flowers rise on short stems in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring in full sun to part shade.

directions: Grow in gritty, well-drained soil, splitting clumps in spring to spread it around. The bright glossy-green leaves form a fresh-looking mound rather than a flat mat. Golden-yellow flowers open in early summer and are followed by decorative reddish seed capsules.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in lean, dry, gritty soil.

directions: Grow this European native in poor, sharply drained soil and full sun. The blue-green needle-like leaves are carried on trailing stems that curl upward at the tips. Bright yellow flowers open in nodding clusters in summer, and the mat tolerates heat and neglect.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty soil and full sun.

directions: Set into lean, well-drained soil where the fine blue-green foliage forms low tufted mats. Its needle-like leaves cluster into rosette-like tips along creeping stems. Yellow flowers open in summer; this species tolerates a little more shade than most stonecrops.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow in gritty soil and full sun to keep the tall stems upright. Broad, flattened flower heads open pink and mature to deeper rose over blue-green foliage. Leave the dried heads standing into winter and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the darkest foliage.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun to deepen the purple-tinged leaves. Dusky-pink flower heads open in late summer above the dark, upright stems. Keep it unfertilized so the stems stay strong and self-supporting.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring once the soil is workable, in fast-draining gritty soil.

directions: Set rosettes shallowly so the base sits at the soil surface and firm the grit around the roots. Water sparingly and let the mix dry fully between waterings, as standing moisture rots the crown. A mature rosette sends up a star-shaped pink bloom stalk in summer before dying back and leaving its offsets to carry on.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in lean, alkaline, fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant rosettes shallowly in gritty soil and add crushed limestone or grit for the sharp drainage they favor. Water only when the soil is bone dry and avoid rich fertilizer, which dulls the leaf coloring. Blue-green rosettes carry distinctive reddish-brown leaf tips, deepening in strong sun and cold weather.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set rosettes at the soil surface in a very free-draining gritty mix. Give full sun to bring out the strong red winter coloring and keep water off the crown. Rosettes flush deep red in cold months, and mature plants send up rosy summer flower spikes.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty alpine-style soil with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant the small dark-green rosettes shallowly in a gritty alpine mix and firm gently. This high-altitude species resents heat and humidity, so give it airflow and lean soil. Rosettes stay tight and matte green, and unusually large purple flowers open on short stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty soil and protect from winter wet.

directions: Plant rosettes in a very gritty, fast-draining mix and shelter them from prolonged winter wet. The hairy grey-green leaves are prone to rot if moisture lingers in the crown. Fuzzy silvery rosettes form tight domes, and greenish-yellow flowers appear in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Unlike most houseleeks, this species multiplies by splitting its own rosette rather than sending out offset stems, so divide the crown to propagate. Plant in gritty soil with full sun and minimal water. Rosettes hold rich seasonal color, and yellow flowers rise on stout stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in lean, gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the round green rosettes at the surface of a gritty mix and keep them dry between waterings. Loose, ball-shaped offsets detach readily and roll off to root nearby. Bright rosettes produce pale yellow flowers on short stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant rosettes shallowly in a gritty, fast-draining mix and give full sun. Keep moisture off the crown and let the soil dry fully between drinks. Green rosettes carry reddish leaf tips and open pink-purple flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in very lean, gritty soil in full sun.

directions: Plant small rosettes in a lean gritty mix that drains almost instantly. This compact species tolerates poor serpentine soils and needs very little feeding. Grey-green webbed rosettes carry dark tips and produce pale yellow summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty alpine soil with sharp drainage.

directions: Set the open blue-green rosettes into a gritty, free-draining mix in full sun. This alpine dislikes heat and lingering damp, so give it airflow. Rosettes carry a red base to the leaves, and yellow flowers rise on tall stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the loose green rosettes shallowly in a gritty mix and keep them lean. The foliage has a faint sticky, resinous scent when handled. Rosettes bear unusually large yellow star flowers on stout stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant compact rosettes in a gritty mix with excellent drainage and full sun. This mountain species stays small and tight and colors deeply in cold. Green rosettes flush red at the tips and produce rosy-pink flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set green rosettes into a gritty, fast-draining mix in a warm sunny spot. This North African species handles heat better than most houseleeks. Rosettes carry faint reddish flushing and open pink flowers on upright stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty alkaline soil with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant the small tight rosettes in a gritty, lime-rich mix in full sun. This compact alpine forms neat clusters and colors red in cold weather. Green rosettes with reddish tips send up rose-pink flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant rosettes shallowly in a gritty mix and give strong sun for best color. Keep the crown dry and the soil lean to hold the compact form. Green rosettes carry purple-red leaf tips and produce reddish-purple flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the small green rosettes in a gritty, fast-draining mix in full sun. This Balkan species stays compact and forms dense mats over time. Rosettes carry reddish bases and open pink-purple flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set the medium green rosettes into a gritty mix and keep them lean. Full sun and dry conditions bring out subtle reddish leaf tips. Rosettes send up pale yellow flowers on upright stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant compact rosettes in a gritty, fast-draining mix in full sun. Keep water off the crown and let the soil dry fully between waterings. Green rosettes flush red toward the tips and produce yellow flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the large green rosettes shallowly in a gritty, free-draining mix. Give full sun to develop the deep red leaf tips that mark this form. Robust rosettes multiply freely and send up pink star flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Set rosettes at the soil surface in a gritty mix and grow in full sun for strongest color. Feed sparingly, since rich soil mutes the red tipping. Green rosettes carry bold crimson leaf tips and send up pink summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant offsets shallowly in a gritty mix and keep them dry between waterings. Full sun and cool weather deepen the rich red coloring. Glossy red rosettes multiply into tidy clumps and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Set the offsets in a gritty, fast-draining mix and give the strongest sun available. Lean soil and cool nights intensify the near-black tips. Green rosettes with dramatic dark maroon centers send up pink flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant offsets shallowly in gritty soil and water only when fully dry. Bright light brings out the deep ruby-red rosette color. Compact wine-red rosettes form neat clusters and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant offsets in a gritty, free-draining mix and keep the crown dry. Full sun holds the frosted blue tone through the growing season. Silvery blue-green rosettes form tidy clumps and produce pink summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty soil, protected from winter wet.

directions: Plant the small webbed rosettes in a gritty mix and keep moisture off the fine hairs. Sharp drainage prevents rot in the tight cobwebbing. Petite silvery-webbed rosettes cluster densely and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set offsets shallowly in gritty soil and grow in full sun to firm the unusual foliage. Keep water off the crown and feed lightly. This novelty forms tubular, rolled leaves tipped in red rather than flat ones, and blooms pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant offsets in a gritty mix and keep them lean and dry for best color. Strong sun brings out the bright seasonal tones. Rosettes shift through green and rosy-red across the seasons and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty soil, sheltered from winter wet.

directions: Plant the webbed rosettes in a gritty, fast-draining mix and keep the crown dry. Sharp drainage protects the fine cobweb hairs from rot. Lavender-purple rosettes laced with white webbing cluster tightly and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty soil, protected from winter wet.

directions: Plant the heavily webbed rosettes in a gritty mix with excellent drainage. Keep water off the dense hairs to prevent crown rot. Small rosettes wrapped in thick white webbing form tight buns and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set offsets in a gritty mix and give full sun to hold the gold coloring. Cool weather adds red tones over the yellow base. Golden-yellow rosettes blush red in cold and produce pink flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant the large offsets in a gritty, free-draining mix in full sun. This vigorous old cultivar forms some of the biggest rosettes in the genus. Broad red rosettes with green tips multiply strongly and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant offsets shallowly in a gritty mix and keep them dry between waterings. Full sun deepens the purple flush across the rosette. Purple-red rosettes form tidy mats and send up pink flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Set offsets in a gritty, fast-draining mix and grow in strong sun. Lean soil and cool weather bring out the ruby tones. Deep red rosettes with a silvery sheen cluster neatly and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant offsets in a gritty mix and give full sun to keep the silvery cast. Keep the crown dry and the soil lean. Frosty silver-green rosettes take on rose tints in cold and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant offsets in a gritty mix and grow in full sun to hold the honey tones. Cool weather adds a red edge to the golden leaves. Warm golden-yellow rosettes blush red in winter and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set offsets in a gritty mix and grow in full sun for the crisp two-tone effect. Keep the crown dry and feed sparingly. Green rosettes rimmed with red tips form tidy clumps and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant the large offsets in a gritty, free-draining mix in full sun. This bold cultivar makes wide rosettes that color deeply in cold. Big deep-red rosettes multiply into broad clumps and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant offsets in a gritty mix and give full sun for the darkest color. Lean, dry conditions intensify the near-black rosette centers. Very dark maroon rosettes form neat clusters and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant offsets in a gritty, free-draining mix in full sun. Keep the crown dry and the soil lean to hold the tidy symmetry. Neat green rosettes with fine red tips form even wheels and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty soil, sheltered from winter wet.

directions: Plant the fine-leaved rosettes in a gritty mix and keep the crown dry. Sharp drainage suits the narrow, bristly foliage. Small spiky rosettes with red tips form dense hummocks and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant offsets in a gritty, fast-draining mix and grow in full sun. Keep water off the crown and feed lightly for best leaf detail. Green rosettes fringed with fine ciliate hairs and red tips form tidy mats and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant the small offsets in a gritty mix and give full sun to warm the color. Lean, dry conditions bring out the terracotta tones. Compact orange-brown rosettes form neat clumps and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set offsets in a gritty, free-draining mix and grow in full sun for the crisp edge color. Keep the crown dry and the soil lean. Even green rosettes with dark chocolate leaf tips form tidy symmetric buttons and bloom pink in summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in early spring; a few weeks of cold, moist chilling improves sprouting.

directions: Sow seed barely covered in a gritty, free-draining mix and keep it evenly moist and cool until sprouts appear. Set plants in full sun in soil that stays moist but never waterlogged, and shear back the spent flower spikes to tidy the clump. Slender spikes of pink cup-shaped blooms open through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Best sown in spring after a spell of cold stratification.

directions: Chill the seed cold and moist for a few weeks, then sow shallowly and keep cool until it sprouts. This meadow native likes full sun and reliable moisture through the growing season. Tall spires of rose-pink blooms appear in early to midsummer and draw native bees.

planting.note: Sow in spring; the tall stems may need support in rich soil.

directions: Barely cover seed in a moist mix and keep it cool and damp while it germinates. Give this large species full sun, deep soil, and even watering, staking the tall flower stems where wind is a factor. Pale pink to white flowers crowd the upright spikes through early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in spring; tolerates seasonally moist, alkaline soils.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in a moist mix and hold it at cool room temperature until sprouts show. This adaptable species handles spring-wet meadow ground and full sun once established. Deep pink flowers open along slender stems in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring; thrives in damp coastal and streamside ground.

directions: Press seed into a moist mix and keep it cool and steadily damp while it sprouts. This moisture-loving native prefers full sun to light shade and soil that never dries out. Dense spikes of glossy rose-pink flowers stand tall in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Raise from spring-sown seed in consistently moist meadow soil.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep the mix cool and evenly moist until germination. Grow in full sun in rich, damp soil, giving the tall stems room to stand upright. Slim spikes of pink flowers bloom in early summer and support native pollinators.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; propagate this cultivar by division since seed will not come true.

directions: Set divisions in full sun in fertile, free-draining soil and water them in well. Cut the flower stems back after the first flush to encourage a lighter second showing. Softly fringed, satin-pink flowers line the tall spikes through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Divide in spring to keep the deep colour true to type.

directions: Plant divisions in full sun in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and water them in. Trim back the flowered stems to tidy the plant and prompt a modest second flush. Rich purple-red flowers stand out along the tall spikes in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Divide in spring; grown from plants rather than seed.

directions: Plant divisions in full sun in rich, free-draining soil and firm them in well. Shear the stems after flowering to encourage tidy regrowth. Clear satin-pink flowers open along the tall spikes at midsummer.

planting.note: Propagate by division in spring; a compact selection for the front border.

directions: Set divisions in full sun in well-drained soil and water them in while rooting. This dwarf form stays neat and self-supporting near the border edge. Deep pink flowers pack the short spikes through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Divide in spring; a low, tidy form for containers and edging.

directions: Plant divisions in full sun in free-draining soil and keep them evenly moist to establish. The compact habit suits pots and the front of a bed without staking. Soft pink flowers rise on short spikes in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Keep true by spring division of the clump.

directions: Set divisions in full sun in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and water them in. Cut back the finished spikes to keep the plant tidy through late summer. Delicate pale pink flowers line the tall stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: A seed strain sown in spring; expect a range of pink shades.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in a moist mix and keep it cool until germination; a cold spell beforehand helps. Grow the young plants in full sun with steady moisture and deadhead to extend bloom. This mixed strain gives spikes in shades of rose and pink from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once hard frosts have passed.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before, spacing clumps well apart for air movement. Water through the first season while roots establish, then let it fend for itself in average soil. Look for arching sprays of tiny gold flowers that spray outward like a firework display from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Surface-sow in fall so winter cold breaks seed dormancy.

directions: Press seed onto the soil surface without covering it, since light aids germination after a cold winter. Give it room in moist to average ground and it will form a spreading colony. Plumes of golden flowers open on wiry stems in late summer, feeding migrating pollinators.

planting.note: Scatter seed on bare soil in autumn for natural cold conditioning.

directions: Sow seed thinly on the surface in fall and let the seeds overwinter in place. This vigorous native spreads by rhizome, so give it space or a spot where wandering is welcome. Broad pyramids of gold bloom from late summer, drawing bees and butterflies before frost.

planting.note: Fall-sow so seed experiences the moist chill it needs.

directions: Surface-sow into moist ground in autumn and thin seedlings the following spring. It tolerates damp soils and rises tall, so place it toward the back of a border. Arching golden plumes appear atop smooth stems in late summer through early fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last hard frost.

directions: Set divisions at their original depth in average, well-drained soil. This tidy selection stays low with heart-shaped basal foliage and needs no staking. Arching sprays of small golden flowers cover the mound from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Sow in fall for the earliest goldenrod blooms next year.

directions: Surface-sow onto dry, well-drained soil in autumn. As its name suggests, it opens earlier than most goldenrods, often by midsummer. Smooth stems bear arching plumes of gold, extending the season for pollinators.

planting.note: Sow in autumn to satisfy the seed's chilling need.

directions: Surface-sow into average garden soil in fall. This clump-forming European species stays tidy and is valued in traditional herb gardens. Slender spikes of golden-yellow flowers open from mid summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in average, well-drained soil. This compact selection is prized for the front of the border and for containers. Soft lemon-yellow plumes cover the low mound from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Set divisions at grade in well-drained soil and water until established. This tough selection handles heat and drought once settled. Tapering plumes of soft yellow flowers arch outward in late summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth in average soil and keep watered the first season. This compact hybrid holds its flowers in flat, horizontal branching sprays. Bright golden-yellow heads radiate outward from midsummer into fall and make good cut flowers.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost for first-year bloom.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of a warm indoor flat, as it can flower the first year from an early start. Move seedlings out after frost into average, well-drained ground. Dense golden plumes rise on upright stems from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring once frosts have ended.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in average soil and water through establishment. This classic award-winning hybrid recalls the fluffy look of florist mimosa. Conical heads of yellow flowers on stems with crimped foliage open in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Set divisions at their original depth in well-drained soil. This garden selection of a rare native forms an upright clump with cascading flower sprays. Golden plumes arch outward in late summer and fall, drawing in bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Fall-sow into gritty, well-drained soil for cold treatment.

directions: Surface-sow onto lean, sharply drained soil in fall. Unlike its yellow kin, this species carries clean white aster-like flowers. Flat clusters of white bloom top the stems in late summer, thriving in rocky, dry sites.

planting.note: Sow in fall into moist ground for winter chilling.

directions: Press seed onto damp soil in autumn and leave uncovered. This wet-prairie native tolerates seasonally soggy ground and folded, arching leaves distinguish it. Flat-topped golden flower clusters appear in early fall.

planting.note: Fall-sow into moist soil for the required cold period.

directions: Surface-sow into moist to wet ground in fall. This clump-forming native does well at pond edges and in rain gardens. Broad flat-topped heads of gold flowers open on smooth stems in early autumn.

planting.note: Sow in fall into damp soil to break dormancy.

directions: Scatter seed on wet or moist soil in fall and leave on the surface. Its large basal leaves feel like sandpaper, and it thrives in swampy, part-shaded spots. Arching sprays of golden flowers appear in late summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Set divisions at their original depth in average, well-drained soil. This intergeneric cross of goldenrod and aster bears looser, softer flower heads. Sprays of pale primrose-yellow daisy-like flowers open from mid to late summer and last well when cut.

planting.note: Fall-sow into dry, well-drained soil for chilling.

directions: Press seed onto lean, dry soil in autumn. This drought-tough prairie native blooms a bit earlier than many goldenrods. Arching plumes of gold appear from midsummer into fall on smooth stems.

planting.note: Fall-sow into gritty soil for a strong cold period.

directions: Press seed onto sharply drained, gritty soil in fall. This compact alpine native suits rock gardens and cold, high-elevation sites. Small clusters of golden flowers top short stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall into cool, well-drained ground.

directions: Surface-sow into gritty, well-drained soil in fall. This dwarf mountain native forms a low rosette ideal for rock gardens and troughs. Compact golden flower clusters rise on short stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Set divisions at grade in average, well-drained soil. This compact German selection stays neat at the front of a border. Deep golden-yellow plumes cover the low mound from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frosts have ended.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth in average soil. This dwarf hybrid smothers itself in flower on a compact frame that rarely needs support. A dense sheet of deep gold covers the plant from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Fall-sow into well-drained soil for cold stratification.

directions: Surface-sow onto well-drained soil in fall. This rare native is easy in gardens, forming upright clumps with arching flower branches. Golden plumes open in late summer and early fall and are much visited by pollinators.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring so they root before summer heat.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew before in fast-draining soil and water sparingly once established. This selection rarely sends up flower stalks, so it stays a dense, low silver groundcover all season. Shear away any tired or spotted leaves in spring to keep the mat looking fresh.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring or early fall in well-drained ground.

directions: Space this bold form generously since its leaves run twice the size of ordinary lamb's ear. Also sold as 'Helene von Stein' or 'Countess Helen von Stein,' it seldom blooms and holds its soft gray leaves through heat and humidity better than most. Pull off any flower stalks that appear to keep energy in the foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and lean soil.

directions: Set plants in gritty, well-drained soil and keep water off the leaves. Instead of open flowers, the summer stalks carry rounded woolly tufts that look like scattered cotton bolls and dry well for arrangements. Cut the stalks when the fuzzy balls are fully formed for indoor use.

planting.note: Set out in spring where the fresh foliage gets full morning light.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained soil and go easy on water and feeding. New spring growth flushes chartreuse to yellow before aging to the usual soft silver, and short purple flower spikes appear in early summer. Trim spent leaves in spring to show off the bright new rosettes.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a dry, sunny bed.

directions: Give this selection full sun and sharp drainage, spacing plants so air moves freely between the woolly leaves. It is prized for especially dense, soft silver felting and produces short spikes of small purple flowers in early summer. Remove flower stalks after bloom if you prefer a tidy foliage carpet.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Set plants in lean, well-drained soil and avoid wetting the foliage. This variegated form carries irregular creamy-white streaks across its silver leaves, and it can throw plain shoots that should be pulled to keep the pattern. Short purple flower spikes appear in early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for bloom the first summer.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed under bright light and keep it lightly moist until it sprouts. This compact, seed-grown selection makes neat silvery rosettes and can flower its first year, carrying short spikes of lavender-pink blooms. Give it full sun and dry, gritty soil for the best leaf color.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted starts in spring for summer bloom.

directions: Set plants in average, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. This award-winning selection forms a uniform mound of glossy foliage and sends up sturdy spikes packed with rosy-lavender flowers in midsummer. Deadhead or shear after the main flush, then leave the seed heads for winter interest.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and moderately fertile soil.

directions: Give this cultivar full sun and steady but not soggy moisture. Tall, well-branched stems carry soft pink flower spikes above a low mound of dark leaves through summer. Removing the earliest spent stalks encourages more bloom over a long season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun or light shade.

directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil and keep it lightly moist through the growing season. Slender spikes of clear rose-pink flowers rise above neat clumps of scalloped green leaves in early summer. Shear off finished spikes to tidy the plant and coax a few more blooms.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring in full sun to part shade.

directions: Set plants in fertile, well-drained soil in sun or dappled light. Clean white flower spikes stand above tidy green foliage in early to midsummer, brightening a border edge. Cut spent stems back to keep the clump compact.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant out after frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep it cool and moist while it germinates. Rounded, deeply veined leaves form a low rosette beneath whorled spikes of large rosy-purple flowers in early summer. Deadhead to prolong bloom and divide crowded clumps every few years in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun or light shade.

directions: Give this selection fertile, well-drained soil and even moisture. It carries especially rich violet-purple flower whorls above crinkled green foliage from late spring into summer. Shear spent spikes to tidy the clump and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for early summer flowers.

directions: Plant in average garden soil that drains well and stays lightly moist. This vigorous form makes a dense mound of textured leaves topped with sturdy spikes of pinkish-purple flowers. Remove finished stalks and lift and divide the clump when it grows crowded.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or set out after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep it warm and lightly moist until it sprouts. This Southwestern native blooms the first year, sending up loose spikes of tubular scarlet-red flowers that hummingbirds work all season. Give it sun and sharp drainage, and cut it back after the first flush to keep new flowers coming.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or starts after the last frost has passed.

directions: Set plants in full sun and lean, well-drained soil where they can sprawl. The soft gray-green leaves release a fizzy citrus scent when brushed, giving the plant its 7-Up nickname, and coral to salmon-red flowers open from summer into fall. In colder zones treat it as a tender plant or overwinter cuttings indoors.

planting.note: Start indoors or set out in spring in reliably moist ground.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep the mix consistently damp until it germinates. This wetland species thrives in moist to boggy soil at a pond edge, spreading by creeping roots into a colony. Whorled spikes of dull pink-purple flowers appear through summer; site it where its spreading habit is welcome.

planting.note: Start indoors or plant out in spring in a shaded, moist spot.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep evenly moist; the seed germinates readily in cool conditions. This woodland-edge species tolerates shade and carries spikes of dark reddish-purple flowers over heart-shaped leaves that smell strong when crushed. It self-sows freely, so cut back seed heads if you want to limit spread.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or set out after frost in a dry, sunny bed.

directions: Surface-sow the seed under light and keep it barely moist until it sprouts. This low, mounding species from rocky mountain slopes carries narrow gray-green leaves and clusters of soft pink flowers in early summer. Give it full sun and gritty, sharply drained soil, and avoid winter wet.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted clumps in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil surface in a spot with excellent drainage. This classic selection carries unusually large, flat lavender-blue flowers on short stems. Deadhead regularly through summer to keep the compact clumps blooming, and divide every three or four years to renew vigor.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in a warm, freely draining bed.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained ground and avoid mulching directly over the crown. This form is prized for its extra-large lavender-blue heads, some reaching four inches across. Remove faded flowers to prolong the display, which peaks in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring in a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and keep roots on the dry side over winter. This rare yellow-flowered form opens soft primrose to pale butter heads instead of the usual blue. Cut back spent stems to keep the plant blooming through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the fullest range of flower color.

directions: Site in bright sun and lean, well-drained soil to bring out the shifting flower tones. Blooms open white and age through lavender to deep purple, so a single plant shows several colors at once. Deadhead through the season to keep fresh buds coming.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a warm, freely draining bed.

directions: Set the crown flush with the soil and mulch lightly, keeping material off the center. This selection carries deep rosy-purple flowers with paler centers on strong stems. Remove faded heads to stretch the bloom season across summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to deepen the flower and foliage color.

directions: Give this cultivar full sun and sharp drainage for the strongest color. Flowers open lavender and deepen to violet-purple as the season cools, and the foliage can take on purple tints. Deadhead to prolong bloom and cut the clump back in late fall.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in freely draining soil and avoid winter wet. This white-flowered form lights up the border with creamy, fringed heads. Deadhead spent blooms to keep the plant tidy and flowering through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Set clumps at soil level and keep the crown free of heavy mulch. This pure white form pairs well with blue selections in a mixed planting. Shear off finished flowers to encourage a longer season of bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant with the crown even with the ground and provide excellent drainage. This uncommon pink-flowered form offers soft rose to lilac-pink heads. Remove spent blooms to keep buds forming across the warm season.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Give this selection open sun and gritty soil for the richest color. Its flowers are among the deepest, in strong blue to violet-blue tones. Deadhead through the summer and divide crowded clumps in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring and allow room for this unusually tall form to arch.

directions: This tall wild-collected selection can reach waist height, so site it toward the back of a border. Give it full sun and good drainage; light staking may help in rich soils. Pale lavender-blue flowers rise on branching stems through summer, and shearing renews the show.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Set the crown at grade and keep winter moisture away from the roots. This form opens large creamy-white heads with a subtle ivory tone. Deadhead through summer to keep the compact clumps flowering freely.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf form in spring near the front of a sunny bed.

directions: Place this compact selection where its low mounds edge a path or border. Give it full sun and free drainage for the tidiest habit. Lavender-blue flowers appear through summer, and light shearing keeps the plant neat and blooming.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in a warm, well-drained site.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the soil and avoid soggy winter conditions. This German selection bears full lavender-blue heads on strong stems. Remove faded flowers to prolong bloom and divide older clumps to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after a cold-moist chill and set plants out around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it lightly into cool, moist compost, giving it several weeks of chill to break dormancy. Keep the fluffy blue-green foliage in humus-rich soil that never fully dries. Expect airy purple-mauve puffs of stamens in late spring, usually in the second season from seed.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set divisions with the crown at soil level in rich, evenly moist ground. Support the tall stems in exposed sites and cut back spent flowers to tidy the clump. Look for dense, deep rosy-purple stamen clusters in late spring above lacy foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moisture-retentive soil where the dark stems can be admired.

directions: Position the crown at ground level in humus-rich, consistently moist soil. The near-black upright stems carry fluffy lavender-purple flower heads and rarely need staking in sheltered spots. Bloom peaks in early summer, with the dark stems remaining a feature all season.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after chilling, then plant out near the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it cool and moist through a stratifying period before warmth. Grow in fertile, well-drained but never parched soil, and stake the airy stems in windy gardens. Clouds of tiny lilac flowers with pale stamens dance on wiry stems from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this sterile double is increased only by division.

directions: Because it sets no seed, propagate only by careful spring division of the crown into rooted pieces. Give it rich, moisture-retentive soil and unobtrusive support for the tall flowering stems. Sprays of fully double lilac pompons appear from midsummer, lasting well as they lack a fading center.

planting.note: Sow chilled seed indoors and transplant out around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto cool, damp compost and stratify before bringing into gentle warmth. Plant in fertile soil that stays evenly moist and shelter the stems from strong wind. This white-flowered form scatters tiny pale blooms with lemon stamens across the plant from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or divisions in spring in a sheltered, moist border.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in deep, humus-rich ground and provide discreet staking for the very tall stems. Keep the roots cool and moist through summer for the longest display. This vigorous selection carries large, long-lasting sprays of lilac-mauve flowers from early summer well into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions or containers in spring in fertile, retentive soil.

directions: Plant with the crown level in rich soil and stake the towering stems early in the season. Keep well watered in dry spells so the long bloom period is not cut short. Airy clouds of pure white flowers with soft yellow centers float above the foliage from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Start chilled seed indoors and set out around the last frost date.

directions: Barely cover seed on cool moist compost and stratify to improve germination. Grow in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in dappled light where the tall stems are protected from wind. Wiry purple stems bear a haze of lavender flowers with drooping yellow stamens through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start chilled seed indoors and plant out near the last frost.

directions: Sow lightly covered seed after a cold-moist period and grow on until sturdy. This form prizes its blue-gray foliage, which shows best in full sun with steady moisture. Sulphur-yellow fluffy flower heads rise on strong stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant this tall hybrid in spring in deep, rich soil with room overhead.

directions: Give this giant hybrid deep, fertile, moisture-retentive soil and a sheltered position, as stems can top eight feet. Divide in spring to increase it since seed does not come true. Blue-gray foliage flushed purple gives way to airy panicles of soft lilac-yellow flowers in early summer.

planting.note: Plant small divisions in spring in a shady rock garden or trough.

directions: Set the tiny creeping rootstock in cool, humus-rich, well-drained soil in light shade. It slowly spreads into a low mat ideal for troughs, rock gardens, or woodland edges. Miniature fluffy lilac-pink flowers appear over ferny foliage from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in fall or spring after a period of chilling.

directions: Scatter seed on well-drained soil in sun or light shade, tolerating drier ground than most meadow rues. Grown mainly for its delicate maidenhair-like foliage, it stays compact and airy. Small nodding greenish-yellow flowers hang from the stems in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall in moist soil for winter stratification.

directions: Sow seed in fall in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in full sun to part shade. Named for its narrow, glossy leaflets, it thrives in damp meadow conditions. Fluffy greenish-yellow flower heads open in mid to late summer above the shining foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shady woodland spot with rich soil.

directions: Set the creeping rootstock in cool, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade or dappled light. Grown as much for its rounded, often silver-marbled leaves as its flowers, it slowly forms a low patch. Sprays of tiny lilac flowers with pale stamens appear in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or divisions in spring in fertile, moist soil.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in rich, evenly moist ground in sun to part shade. This compact hybrid needs little staking and returns reliably each year. Clouds of small white flowers with soft yellow stamens open over blue-green foliage across summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or containers in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant with the crown level in humus-rich soil kept steadily moist through the season. This tidy, free-flowering hybrid rarely needs support and comes back dependably. Airy sprays of soft rosy-pink flowers rise above lacy blue-green leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant this tall double in spring in deep, rich, moist soil.

directions: Give this vigorous hybrid deep, fertile, moisture-retentive soil and a sheltered spot for its tall stems. Divide in spring to propagate, since the double flowers set little viable seed. Large airy panicles of double lavender-mauve blooms appear from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in fall to give seed its winter chill.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, fertile soil in full sun to light shade. This upright species has an erect, narrow habit that suits naturalistic borders and meadows. Slender panicles of nodding pale-yellow flowers open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant the small tubers in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the slender tubers a couple of inches deep in sharply drained, humus-rich soil. This unusual dwarf species can go summer-dormant, so avoid overwatering when it rests. Comparatively large creamy-white cup-shaped flowers open in early summer above ferny foliage.

planting.note: Sow in fall in moist woodland soil for natural stratification.

directions: Sow seed in fall in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in part shade. This east-Asian species forms upright clumps and appreciates cool, humus-rich conditions. Airy heads of creamy-white flowers with prominent stamens appear in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed outdoors in spring after the ground warms, or start indoors and set out young plants.

directions: Nick or soak the hard seed coat before sowing about a quarter inch deep to speed germination. Give plants room and lean soil, and avoid moving established clumps since the deep taproot resents disturbance. Expect tall spikes of clear yellow pea-flowers in late spring, followed by ornamental gray seed pods.

planting.note: Fall-sow so winter cold naturally softens the seed coat, or scarify and sow in spring.

directions: Scatter scarified seed a quarter inch deep in well-drained, gritty soil in full sun. This prairie native spreads slowly by rhizomes to form low colonies, so give it space where its wandering is welcome. Bright yellow flowers open in mid to late spring, among the earliest golden banners to bloom.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot in the wild garden.

directions: Soak or nick the seed and sow a quarter inch deep in loose, gritty soil. This western mountain species tolerates cold and lean ground and forms upright leafy clumps. Erect racemes of yellow flowers appear in late spring and early summer near streambanks and meadows in the wild.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Scarify the tough seed and sow shallowly in gritty, well-drained soil. This hardy steppe species from central and eastern Asia forms tidy silvery-leaved clumps and shrugs off drought once rooted. Short spikes of yellow pea-flowers open in late spring above the foliage.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring in a sunny border with good drainage.

directions: Nick or soak the seed before sowing a quarter inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This eastern Chinese species makes upright leafy stems and settles in best where it can stay put. Yellow lupine-like flower spikes rise in late spring.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring; tolerant of cool coastal conditions.

directions: Soften the hard seed coat and sow shallowly in moist but well-drained soil. Native to coastal northeastern Asia and Alaska, this species handles cool summers and salt-laden air. Clusters of yellow flowers appear in early to midsummer above clover-like leaves.

planting.note: Fall-sow in the West so seed germinates with winter rains, or scarify for spring sowing.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed a quarter inch deep in lean, fast-draining soil in full sun. This California native handles dry summers and needs little water once established. Showy racemes of golden-yellow flowers rise in spring.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring in dappled sun to full sun with good drainage.

directions: Nick the seed and sow shallowly in well-drained woodland or meadow soil. This slender western species tolerates light shade at forest edges and forms airy leafy stems. Loose spikes of yellow flowers open in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring in a sunny, well-drained site in the wild garden.

directions: Soak or scarify the seed and sow a quarter inch deep in gritty soil. Native to the Rocky Mountain foothills and meadows, it spreads gently by rhizomes to naturalize. Upright yellow flower spikes bloom in late spring, giving way to spreading, curved pods.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring in humus-rich, well-drained soil in sun to light shade.

directions: Nick the hard seed and sow shallowly in moist, fertile soil at a woodland edge. This southern Appalachian native forms upright leafy clumps and tolerates part shade well. Yellow pea-flowers open in late spring above bluish-green foliage.
